rationale: This is a classic textbook example of the Ritter reaction. Tert-butanol is protonated by a strong acid (sulfuric acid) and loses water to form a stable tert-butyl carbocation. This carbocation is then trapped by the nucleophilic nitrogen of acetonitrile, forming a nitrilium ion intermediate. Subsequent hydrolysis yields the final product, N-tert-butylacetamide. This example clearly demonstrates the key transformation: the formation of an amide from an alcohol (carbocation precursor) and a nitrile under acidic conditions.
collision_avoidance_note: This example uses simple, acyclic, saturated aliphatic substrates (tert-butanol and acetonitrile) to avoid overlap with other named reactions. It avoids arenes (Friedel-Crafts), alkenes (Markovnikov additions, Prins), carbonyls (Beckmann rearrangement), and other potentially reactive functional groups that could trigger different named reaction classifiers. The core transformation is unambiguously the Ritter reaction.
